coredata.dc:title: Chemiluminescence of silver and nitrogen doped carbon dots induced by potassium ferricyanide/hydrogen peroxide and its analytical application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yamei
Xie, Jiaoyan
Wang, Xiaowei
Wang, Zixuan
Han, Suqin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dots doped with silver and nitrogen (Ag,N-CDs) were synthesized and their application in chemiluminescence (CL) was investigated using the potassium ferricyanide/hydrogen peroxide (K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2) reaction. Theoretical calculations reveal that Ag doping facilitates a lower excitation energy. The experimental conditions influencing the CL reaction were examined and optimized. The resulting Ag,N-CDs-based CL method demonstrated impressive capabilities in detection of Cu(II) within a linear calibration range of 40.0 nM–8.0 μM, with a commendable detection limit of 3.1 nM. In extensive validation experiments, recoveries and relative standard deviations for water, plasma, and tea samples consistently fell within the range of 97.0–103.0 % and 1.03–4.19 %, respectively. These outcomes underscore that the CL method based Ag,N-CDs is an excellent tool for the detection of Cu(II).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag,N doped CDs
Chemiluminescence
Potassium ferricyanide
Hydrogen peroxide
Theoretical calculation
Cu(II)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the FI manifolds for the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 CL detection system. Pump1 and Pump 2 stand for peristaltic pumps for solution delivery. High voltage: −600 V.

(a) TEM images of Ag,N-CDs. (b) Diameter distribution histogram of Ag,N-CDs. (c) EDS spectrum of Ag,N-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of Ag,N-CDs. (e) UV–vis spectrum of Ag,N-CDs (Insert: Photograph of Ag,N-CDs under the excitation of UV light with 365 nm). (f) Excitation-dependent of FL spectra of Ag,N-CDs.

(a) CL intensity of K3 Fe(CN)6/H2O2 (1), Ag,N-CDs/H2O2 (2), Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6 (3), Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 (4) and Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2/Cu(II) (5). (b) Effect of radical scavengers on CL intensity of Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 (black) and Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2/Cu(II) (red). (c) CL spectra of Ag,N-CDs/H2O2 (1), Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6 (2), Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2/Cu(II) (3) and Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 (4).
Hole–electron distribution diagram of the most related excited state of p-CDs (a), N-CDs (b) and Ag,N-CDs (c), respectively. Hole and electron are colored by white and blue respectively.
Synthesis strategy of Ag,N-CDs and the proposed CL sensing for Cu(II) detection.
Comparison of the proposed Cu(II) detection method with other related methods.

head.ce:title: Chemiluminescence of silver and nitrogen doped carbon dots induced by potassium ferricyanide/hydrogen peroxide and its analytical application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030000, Shanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030000, Shanxi, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the effects of Ag,N-CDs on the K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 reaction were investigated. The results showed a remarkable improvement in the weak CL reaction when Ag,N-CDs were used. Theoretical calculations show that Ag doping enables a lower excitation energy. The resulting Ag,N-CDs-based CL method demonstrated impressive capabilities in the detection of Cu(II).

In this study, carbon dots doped with silver and nitrogen (Ag,N-CDs) were synthesized and their application in chemiluminescence (CL) was investigated using the potassium ferricyanide/hydrogen peroxide (K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2) reaction. Theoretical calculations reveal that Ag doping facilitates a lower excitation energy. The experimental conditions influencing the CL reaction were examined and optimized. The resulting Ag,N-CDs-based CL method demonstrated impressive capabilities in detection of Cu(II) within a linear calibration range of 40.0 nM–8.0 μM, with a commendable detection limit of 3.1 nM. In extensive validation experiments, recoveries and relative standard deviations for water, plasma, and tea samples consistently fell within the range of 97.0–103.0 % and 1.03–4.19 %, respectively. These outcomes underscore that the CL method based Ag,N-CDs is an excellent tool for the detection of Cu(II).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) is a phenomenon characterized by the emission of photons during specific chemical reactions when excited molecules transition to their stable states [1]. It is notable for its ability to establish a direct relationship between the emitted light intensity and the analyte concentration. Due to its high sensitivity, broad linear dynamic range, minimal background noise, rapid detection capability, and cost-effectiveness, CL is widely utilized in various fields including bioengineering, pharmaceuticals, molecular biology, clinical diagnostics and environmental analysis [2]. CL systems can be categorized as direct CL (the emitter is a product of the chemical reaction) and sensitized CL (energy transfer occurs between an excited product and a second substance that then becomes the emitter) [3]. However, it’s important to note that CL signals are often inherently weak, making their detection challenging. Various substances, including metal ions, small molecules, and nanomaterials, are commonly employed to enhance CL intensity [4,5]. Of all the types of nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials, particularly carbon nanodots (CDs), have received tremendous attention [6]. It exhibits outstanding features, including chemical inertness, strong electron properties, tunable luminescence, ease of synthesis, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. Their application in CL sensing has surged in recent years [7,8]. The incorporation of heteroatoms through doping, encompassing non-metallic and metallic elements, leads to substantial modifications in their chemical composition, nanostructure, and electronic structure [9,10]. The improvement of optical properties and fluorescence quantum yield (QY) in CDs through the introduction of nonmetallic elements has been extensively documented and studied [11]. Various nonmetallic elements, including N [12], Si [13], B and N binding [14], and N and S binding [15], are utilized to engineer doped CDs-based CL systems that capable of producing customized luminescent signals to meet the demands of personalized applications. Metal ions, due to their larger atomic radii, higher electron affinity, and available unoccupied orbitals compared to most nonmetals, induce significant alterations in the electron distribution and energy gap when doped into CDs [9]. This allows precise tuning of CDs’ structural and chemical features, for example, leading to a narrowing of the energy gap between the ground and excited states. These modifications substantially enhance the physicochemical attributes of CDs, leading to improvements in parameters like QY and catalytic properties [16]. The utilization of metal ions in CDs for CL analysis is less developed. Duan et al. developed the Cu-doped CDs as catalysts of luminol-H2O2 system for the CL detection of glucose [17]. Delnavaz et al. used the Co, N co-doped CDs catalyzed the luminol-NaIO4 reaction for an ultrasensitive CL assay for 4-nitrophenol [18], and they also established a CL method for the determination of the 3-nitrotyrosine based on the H2O2-NaIO4 reaction enhanced by Co,N-doped CDs [19].

Copper is the third most abundant transition metal ion in the human body and plays an important role in various biological processes [20]. It has also been found to be harmful to humans at high concentrations of Cu(II), such as damaging the liver and kidney, and inducing gastrointestinal diseases and cardiovascular diseases [21]. To ensure human health, the World Health Organization (WHO) sets a limit of 30 μM of Cu(II) in drinking water [20]. It is of great importance for the protection of the environment and human health to develop a simple, sensitive and reliable method for the determination of trace Cu(II). Many methods have been developed to detect Cu(II), such as flame atomic absorption spectroscopy (FAAS) [22], electrochemistry [23], colorimetry [24,25] and fluorescence (FL) method [26,27] and CL [28]. Despite the fact that these methods were characterized with accurate and convincing results, the expensive equipment and professional operator still limit their widespread applications in practical fields. Therefore, more appropriate and simple strategies are indispensable for the detection Cu(II).

Inspired by the above observations, herein a facile one-pot hydrothermal synthesis strategy was proposed to prepare silver and nitrogen codoped-CDs (Ag,N-CDs) using fructose, urea, and silver nitrate as precursors, and the QY of Ag,N-CDs was up to 45.8 %. The prepared Ag,N-CDs exhibited superior sensitized performance for the CL reaction of K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2. In addition, trace amounts of Cu(II) were shown to exhibit an inhibitory effect on CL emission. Thus, a CL method for sensitive determination of Cu(II) based on Ag,N-CDs was constructed and was used for selective determination of Cu(II) in water, plasma, and tea samples. The synthesis strategy of Ag,N-CDs, and the proposed CL method for the Cu(II) detection process were illustrated in Scheme 1 .

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water. Fructose, urea and K3Fe(CN)6 were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.; AgNO3, CuSO4 and H2O2 were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Stock solutions of 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 and 0.01 M Cu(II) were prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of corresponding solid in water, respectively. The H2O2 solution was prepared by diluting the 30 % H2O2 solution at the time it will be used.

In this work, Ag,N-CDs were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method according to the literature with certain modifications [29]. First, 0.5 g fructose, 1.0 g urea and 1.5 mL 0.01 M AgNO3 solution were added in 15 mL water, which was completely dissolved by ultrasound. The mixed solution was then transferred to a Teflon-coated autoclave, placed in an oven at 180°C, and maintained for 5h. After the reaction, it was naturally cooled to room temperature and a dark brown solution was obtained. The dark brown solution was filtered, centrifuged and dialyzed, after which a clear, transparent, light brown solution was obtained and was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator. The same method was used to produce pristine CDs (p-CDs) with fructose as precursor and N-doped CDs (N-CDs) with fructose and urea as precursors. The amount of raw materials and reaction conditions were investigated with respect to the effect of QY of Ag,N-CDs, and the result is showed in Fig. S1.

The flow injection (FI)-CL process roadmap used in this work is shown in Fig. 1 . Two peristaltic pumps and a six-way injection valve were used to deliver all solutions. The solution of Ag,N-CDs and H2O2 were pumped into the injection valve by peristaltic pump 1 and driven through carrier water into flow cell. K3Fe(CN)6 in NaOH and Cu(II) standard/sample solutions were transported by the peristaltic pump 2 with a flow rate of 2.7 mL min−1. The determination of Cu(II) was based on the changes of CL intensity, ΔI = I 0 − I, where I 0 and I were CL signals in the absence and presence of Cu(II), respectively. The CL signals were monitored by a photomultiplier tube (PMT, −600 V) near the flowing CL reaction cell, and were transferred to the computer for data collection. The peak height of the recorded signal was measured as CL intensity.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). The energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) was recorded by a JSM-7500F scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured with a Varian 660-IR spectrometer (Varian, USA). UV–vis absorption spectrum was scanned using a TU-1810 PC spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). FL spectrum was collected on an F-380 FL spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China). The CL experimentations were carried out on a BPCL ultra-weak luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China), which has a model IFIS-C intelligent flow injection sampler (Xi’an Remax Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., Shannxi, China).

Density functional theory (DFT) and Time-Dependent Density Functional Theory (TDDFT) calculations were performed by using Gaussian 09 [30]. The CAM-B3LYP [31] hybrid functional combined with the triple-ζ def2-TZVP [32] basis set was used for the calculations. The SMD solvation model [33] was employed to consider the solvation effect for all calculations. Multiwfn [34] and VMD [35] were used for visualization.

The morphology and size of the prepared Ag,N-CDs were observed by TEM (Fig. 2 a). The Ag,N-CDs exhibited a spherical shape with a narrow range, 4.6–6.6 nm and an average diameter of 5.6 nm (Fig. 2b). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2a, inset) showed the lattice parameter of the Ag,N-CDs, which was measured to be 0.22 nm, which correspond to the diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon [17]. The small size of the nanoparticles enabled excellent dispersion in aqueous solutions, indicating their ability for applications in CL analysis. The EDS spectrum (Fig. 2c) indicates successful doping of Ag element in the CDs, as evidenced by the peak at 3.0 keV, attributed to Ag [36]. The FT-IR spectrum was used to verify the functional groups on the surface of the Ag,N-CDs (Fig. 2d). A broad OH/NH stretching vibration was observed at 3432 cm−1, and the sharp peak at 1637 cm−1 was attributed to the CO stretch vibrations. The peaks at 2927 cm−1 and 2856 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–H groups. The absorption peaks at 1400, 1272, 1116 and 1020 cm−1 may be caused by the stretching vibration of amide and CN [29]. The result indicates the presence of numerous oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of Ag,N-CDs, which can provide binding sites for jioning in the CL reaction of the K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system and further sensing assays for Cu(II), giving the Ag,N-CDs excellent water solubility.

The optical performance of Ag,N-CDs was monitored by UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL spectrum. In UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2e), there was a distinct absorption peak at 300 nm and displayed bright blue FL under UV (365 nm) irradiation. As shown in Fig. 2f, the excitation-dependent emission behaviours was observed in the range from 360 to 440 nm, which is one of most special natures of CDs [37,38].

The CL reactions of Ag,N-CDs with several common oxidants were first studied and compared by the FI mode. As shown in Fig. S2a, acidic KMnO4 and alkaline K3Fe(CN)6 produced the stronger CL intensity. However, the CL ntensity remains inadequate for practical use and fails to meet sensitivity standards. Therefore, enhancing the system’s CL intensity is imperative. Introducing H2O2 into the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6 system led to significant increases in CL intensity, while a slight decrease in CL signal was observed adding H2O2 to the Ag,N-CDs/KMnO4 system (Fig. S2b).

The CL process was further investigated using the static injection method. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system and the Ag,N-CDs/H2O2 system exhibited extremely weak CL intensity, the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6 system demonstrated a higher CL intensity, while the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system exhibited the strongest CL intensity among all tested systems. Therefore, the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system was selected for further study. A control experiment was conducted using the same reagents employed in the preparation of Ag,N-CDs (fructose, urea, and silver nitrate mixture). No evident CL was observed in this solution, confirming that the CL enhancement originated from the Ag,N-CDs. It is noteworthy that the addition of Cu(II) resulted in a decrease in signal. Therefore, we further investigated the performance of the CL system proposed above for Cu(II) detection. In Fig. S3a, the CL performance of p-CDs, N-CDs and Ag,N-CDs in the K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system was compared. Upon addition of Ag,N-CDs, the CL intensity in the K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system reached its peak. The effect of reagent mixing sequences on the CL intensity of the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system was investigated. As shown in Fig. S3b, injecting Ag,N-CDs into the mixture of K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 (1) or injecting H2O2 into Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6 (2) did not significantly increase the CL intensity. However, injecting K3Fe(CN)6 into the Ag,N-CDs/H2O2 mixing solution (3) resulted in the maximum CL intensity observed at 1.5 s. The phenomenon of no significant CL increase in modes 1 and 2 could be due to the fact that the reaction occurred between K3Fe(CN)6 and H2O2 before adding Ag,N-CDs and between Ag,N-CDs and K3Fe(CN)6 before combining H2O2. In contrast, as in mode 3, almost no reaction was generated between Ag,N-CDs and H2O2 before the addition of K3Fe(CN)6 [39]. Therefore, mode (3), i.e. the injecting K3Fe(CN)6 into the Ag,N-CDs/H2O2 mixed solution, was chosen to design the flow path for the FI-CL method.

In order to monitor the intermediates formed during the reaction and the role of radicals, the influence of free radical scavengers on the CL strength of the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system was investigated. The results are summarized in Fig. 3b. Ascorbic acid was a classical reducing agent and radical scavenger, and its presence strongly inhibited the CL intensity, confirming that the CL reaction proceeds via the free radical pathway. Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT), a scavenger of superoxide anion (•O2 –), had a stronger inhibitory effect on CL intensity, confirming the existence of •O2 –. Thiourea was an effective •OH scavenger, and its quenching effect on CL intensity confirmed the production of •OH. The substance that removed 1O2 was sodium azide (NaN3), which led to a quenching of CL intensity, confirming the presence of 1O2. Thus, •O2 −, •OH and 1O2 were formed during the CL reaction processes.

In Fig. S3c, FL spectra of Ag,N-CDs at 400 nm excitation wavelength with and without K3Fe(CN)6, H2O2, K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 and K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2/Cu(II) showed that the FL intensity of Ag,N-CDs decreased to some extent after the reaction but the wavelength remained unchanged. This could be due to the fact that some of the Ag,N-CDs were consumed by strong oxidizing free radicals generated during the CL reaction, leading to a decrease in the FL intensity of the Ag,N-CDs. In the meantime, the CL spectra were measured with high-energy cutoff filters of different wavelengths. The obtained CL spectrum (Fig. 3c) showed an emission band in the range of 450–600 nm with maximum at 535 nm, which was uniform with the FL spectrum of Ag,N-CDs and not with the CL spectrum of 1O2 [40]. Therefore, we presumed that the main light emission species came from Ag,N-CDs in the excited state (Ag,N-CDs*) instead of 1O2 in the CL system. On the other hand, 1O2 was also active and easy to form excited (1O2)2*, a dimer aggregate of monomol singlet oxygen. This dimer was very easy to transfer its energy to Ag,N-CDs forming Ag,N-CDs* by an energy transfer process because the emission wavelength of (1O2)2* is in the range of the excitation wavelength of the Ag,N-CDs [41]. In fact, some groups on the outer surface of Ag,N-CDs behaved as the receptor sites through the acid-base pairing interaction, which was very important in the CL energy-transfer process [42]. Compared to the FL spectrum, the CL spectrum showed a certain degree of redshift, which could be explained by the difference between the CL and FL emission states [38,43]. In addition, when Cu(II) was added to the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system, the maximum emission position did not change, but the intensity decreased. Therefore, the final emission of the system comes from Ag,N-CDs.

Based on the above study, the mechanism of the reinforcing effect of Ag,N-CDs for the K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 reaction can be mainly explained by CL energy transfer between 1O2 and Ag,N-CDs, radiative electron hole annihilation between Ag,N-CDs with hole injection (Ag,N-CDs•+) and Ag,N-CDs with electron injection (Ag,N-CDs•−). K3Fe(CN)6 can react with H2O2 to produce •O2 − in basic medium [44], and •O2 − can be digested to produce (1O2)2* [45]. The Ag,N-CDs could accept the energy of (1O2)2* and generate Ag,N-CDs* [40], which returned to the ground state and released photons. In the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system, •OH was formed by the interation between •O2 − and H2O2 [46,47]. K3Fe(CN)6 and •OH could serve as hole injector and convert Ag,N-CDs to Ag,N-CDs•+ [48]. The electron donation from •O2 − to Ag,N-CDs produced Ag,N-CDs•−. Electron-hole annihilation in or between the Ag,N-CDs•+ and Ag,N-CDs•− resulted in energy release with CL emission.

In addition, the inhibitory effect of Cu(II) on the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 CL system was also investigated. The UV–vis spectra of Ag,N-CDs with or without Cu(II) were investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the absorption peak of Ag,N-CDs at 275 nm disappeared in the mixed solution of Cu(II) and Ag,N-CDs, indicating that Cu(II) reacted with Ag,N-CDs. The FL spectra in Fig. S3c also showed that the presence of Cu(II) caused some reduction in the FL intensity of Ag,N-CDs. This result was not very surprising as Cu(II) is a well-known highly efficient FL quencher due to its paramagnetic property by electron or energy transfer [49,50]. Based on the above results, the quenching effect of Cu(II) on the CL intensity of the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system was reasoned that Cu(II) could interact with Ag,N-CDs and lead to the consumption of Ag,N-CDs, resulting in a decrease in CL intensity.

DFT and TDDFT calculations were conducted to investigate the enhanced CL of the Ag,N-CDs, comparing them with p-CDs and N-CDs. Given the significant influence of functional groups and N-doping position on the photochemistry of CDs [51,52], no functional groups were included in this study. The exploration focused on limited variations in the position of N-doping and Ag,N co-doping, with reference to Wu et al [53]. The optimized structures of the ground state of p-CDs, N-CDs, and Ag,N-CDs are depicted in Fig. S4. Frontier orbital analysis of the ground state p-CDs, N-CDs, and Ag,N-CDs (Fig. S5) reveals the introduction of N and Ag with lower energy orbitals, allowing a more accessible reaction channel for the p-CDs to be excited. The calculated excitation energies of the most related excited states are presented in Table S1, with the corresponding absorption spectrum shown in Fig. S6. The excitation energy of p-CDs, N-CDs, and Ag,N-CDs are 3.35, 1.52 and 0.85 eV, respectively, indicating that the introduction of the Ag atom in N-CDs yields smaller excitation energies compared to N-CDs and p-CDs alone. The distribution diagram (Fig. 4 ) of the electron and hole in the lowest excited state illustrates that the addition of N and Ag can enhance the separation of electron and hole upon excitation of the system. The addition of Ag can further facilitate the process by providing electrons.

As shown in Fig. 3, Cu(II) can reduce the CL intensity of the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system. To obtain a higher sensitivity for the detection of Cu(II), several experimental variables such as Ag,N-CDs, K3Fe(CN)6, H2O2 and NaOH on the effect of CL intensity were investigated and optimized (Fig. S8). The results showed that the relative CL intensity (ΔI) was highest when using 1:15 (v/v) Ag,N-CDs, 0.15 M H2O2, and 0.6 mM K3Fe(CN)6 in 0.2 M NaOH. Under these conditions, the CL intensity of the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system with different Cu(II) concentrations was shown in Fig. S9a. The intensity of inhibition CL had a good linear relationship with the logarithm of Cu(II) concentration in the range of 0.04 µM to 8.0 μM (Fig. S9b). The linear regression equation was ΔI = 1499.0 + 707.1 lg[Cu(II)] with the correlation coefficient of 0.9974. The limit of detection (LOD) was to be 3.1 nM, which was calculated by 3s b/k, k showed the calibration slope, and s b was the standard deviation of CL signals for eleven repeated determinations of blank solutions. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.62 % (intraday) and 1.87 % (interday, repeatedly for five consecutive days), respectively. The results indicated that the method had good linearity and high accuracy. Compared with other methods, our proposed method showed a lower or comparable detection limit for the detection of Cu(II), as shown in Table 1 . Moreover, this method extended the application of doped CDs in CL systems.

The effect of some species including cations, anions, and small organic molecules was investigated on the determination of 0.6 μM Cu(II) (Table S2). The tolerable concentration ratio for foreign species was taken as the largest amount yielding a relative error less than 5 % and the results demonstrated that none of the compounds interfered with the determination of Cu(II). Thus, the novel CL system demonstrated excellent selectivity for detecting Cu(II).

The CL method was used for the determination of Cu(II) in water, tea, and plasma. To assess the accuracy of the method, recovery experiments were performed and the results are shown in Table S3. The recovery of Cu(II) in the above samples ranged from 97.0 % to 105.0 %, with corresponding RSD ranging from 1.03 % to 4.19 %. The results indicated that the constructed CL method is reliable for the Cu(II) determination in real-life samples.

Ag,N co-doped CDs were successfully synthesized and thoroughly characterized. Both experimental results and DFT calculations confirmed their lower excitation energy and excellent luminescent properties. Based on that, a simple Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 CL system was successfully constructed for selective and sensitive detection of Cu(II). The method was based on the strong specific inhibition effect of Cu(II) on the CL intensity of the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system. The reaction mechanism of the Ag,N-CDs/K3Fe(CN)6/H2O2 system was studied, which may extend the application of the CL system. The present method was applied to the analysis of Cu(II) in tea, plasma and water samples, and the recovery study showed that the results were reasonable.

Yamei Jiang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jiaoyan Xie: Data curation, Conceptualization, Investigation. Xiaowei Wang: Data curation, Conceptualization, Investigation. Zixuan Wang: Writing – review & editing. Suqin Han: Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125791.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. John, Jr. Haas, Chemiluminescent reactions in solution, J. Chem. Edu. 44 (1967) 396–402, doi: 10.1021/ed044p396.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemiluminescence microarrays in analytical chemistry: a critical review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Seidel, R. Niessner, Chemiluminescence microarrays in analytical chemistry: a critical review, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 406 (2014) 5589–5612, doi: 10.1007/s00216-014-7968-4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterial-enhanced chemiluminescence reactions and their applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide-sensitized luminescence and chemiluminescence in the systems containing most often used medicines; a review
